sections.heading: What is PEV?
sections.content:
PEV (Planning → Execution → Validation) is a three-phase development workflow invoked via `/pev`. It separates planning, coding, and documentation review into distinct phases with human approval gates between each.

Each PEV session creates a **cycle manifest doc** (`docs/pev-cycles/{cycle-id}.json`) that tracks the full lifecycle: what was planned, what was built, what was reviewed. This manifest enables resuming interrupted sessions and running parallel PEV cycles without conflicts.

You stay in control: nothing proceeds without your approval, and you can reject any phase's output with feedback to re-run it.

sections.heading: How to Use /pev
sections.content:
Start a PEV session by typing `/pev` followed by your request:

```
/pev Add a history endpoint that supports filtering by date range
```

The orchestrator takes over from there, guiding you through three phases with approval gates.

sections.heading: Agents & Workflow
sections.content:
Four agents work together in a PEV cycle. Each has structurally enforced tool permissions — they literally cannot see or call tools outside their allowlist.

### Agent Summary

| Agent | Role | Tools Available | Cannot Do |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Orchestrator** | Lifecycle, human gates, phase transitions | Create/update cycle manifest, manage tags, `cortex_checkout`, merge worktrees, `cortex_build`, `cortex_check`, commit | Write code, write docs, mark clean |
| **Architect** | Planning — scoped PRD + design spec | Read code/docs (`cortex_source`, `cortex_search`, `cortex_graph`, `cortex_read_doc`, `cortex_render`), write to cycle manifest (`cortex_write_doc`, `cortex_update_section`, `cortex_add_section`), `cortex_build` | Write code (`Edit`, `Write`, `Bash`), touch live feature docs |
| **Builder** | Implementation — code + tests | `Edit`, `Write`, `Bash`, `Read`, `Grep`, `Glob`, read via cortex tools (`cortex_source`, `cortex_search`, `cortex_graph`, `cortex_read_doc`, `cortex_render`) | Write docs, modify cortex DB, commit, `cortex_build` |
| **Auditor** | Validation + post-implementation | Read code (`cortex_source`, `cortex_search`, `cortex_graph`, `cortex_render`, `cortex_diff`), write to feature docs (`cortex_update_section`, `cortex_write_doc`), `cortex_mark_clean`, `cortex_build`, `cortex_check`, `cortex_report`, `cortex_history`, `cortex_add_link`, `cortex_list_undocumented` | Write code (`Edit`, `Write`, `Bash`) |

**Key invariant:** No single agent can both write code AND update documentation.

sections.heading: Phase 1: Planning (Architect Agent)
sections.content:
**What happens:** The Architect explores your codebase and existing docs via Cortex, asks you clarifying questions, and writes a Shape Up-style pitch into the **cycle manifest doc**. This includes:

- Problem statement — what's broken and why
- User stories — 3-5 coarse outcomes that define "done" for this cycle
- Solution sketch — fat-marker description of the approach at module level
- Constraints — rabbit holes to avoid, no-gos, trade-offs

The Architect provides orientation and boundaries, not a prescriptive implementation spec. The Builder reads source code and figures out the implementation details.

**What the Architect does NOT do:** It does not touch your live feature docs (PRD, design spec, capabilities table). Those describe what currently exists and are only updated by the Auditor after code is built. It does not specify function signatures, parameter names, or line-by-line code changes — that's the Builder's job.

**What you see:** The cycle manifest's `scope` (coarse module boundary) and `architect` sections describing what will be built. The Architect will ask you clarifying questions via multiple-choice prompts before writing the pitch. You then approve or reject the pitch before the Builder starts.

sections.heading: The Orchestrator
sections.content:
The Orchestrator is the `/pev` skill itself — it runs in your main session and coordinates the three agent phases. It is not a separate agent with tool restrictions; it has full access to manage the lifecycle.

**Before any phase:**
- Creates the cycle manifest doc and tags it `pev-active`
- Records `baseline_sha` (main HEAD) and start timestamp in the `status` section
- Checks for interrupted cycles from previous sessions (offers resume/release/new)
- On human approval of any phase, updates `status` to the next phase immediately (first action after approval) — this ensures resume works even if the session dies right after approval

**Between phases:**
- Presents each phase's output for your approval
- Manages tag transitions (`planned` → `in_progress` before Builder, `incomplete` on failure)
- Creates git worktree (`git worktree add`), runs `cortex_checkout` (VACUUM INTO) to copy the DB, then dispatches the Builder pointed at the worktree path
- After Builder completes: writes the Builder's returned manifest to the `builder` section, merges the worktree (`git merge --no-commit --no-ff`), removes the worktree — surfaces conflicts, never auto-resolves
- After merge: runs `cortex_build` + `cortex_check` on main, constructs the `change-set` section (planned nodes + actual files changed + deviations)
- Before Auditor dispatch: checks for other active Auditors on main (waits if found)

**Long sessions:** If any agent's context grows too large, a deterministic hook fires and the agent checkpoints its progress. The orchestrator writes the checkpoint to the cycle manifest and dispatches a fresh agent to continue. This happens automatically — you don't need to manage it. Builder incarnations reuse the same worktree (code on disk is preserved).

**After all phases:**
- Creates a single commit (code + docs + changelog together)
- Updates the cycle manifest status to `completed` with commit SHA
- Removes the `pev-active` tag

**On failure:**
- Updates cycle manifest status to `incomplete`
- Removes `pev-active` tag
- The cycle can be resumed on your next `/pev` invocation

sections.heading: Phase 2: Execution (Builder Agent)
sections.content:
**What happens:** The Builder reads the plan from the cycle manifest's `architect` section and implements the code changes in an isolated copy of your repo (a git worktree). The orchestrator creates the worktree, runs `cortex checkout` to copy the cortex DB into it, and then dispatches the Builder pointed at that path. The Builder runs tests and returns an Implementation Manifest — a structured summary of deviations from the spec, files changed, and test results. The orchestrator receives this and writes the `builder` section in the cycle manifest.

**What the Builder can do:** Edit code, run tests, read the codebase via cortex tools (search, source, graph, read_doc).

**What the Builder cannot do:** Modify documentation, update the cortex DB, write to the cycle manifest, or commit. It has no doc-write tools — the "no doc-write" invariant is structural, not prompt-enforced.

**What you see:** Code changes (as a diff), test results, and the manifest showing any deviations with reasons.

**Your options:**
- **Approve** — code is merged into your working directory and proceeds to the Auditor
- **Reject with feedback** — the Builder re-implements with your guidance

sections.heading: Phase 3: Validation (Auditor Agent)
sections.content:
**What happens:** The Auditor re-indexes your project (`cortex build` + `cortex check`), reads the `change-set` section (which the orchestrator constructed from the Architect's scope + Builder's actual changes), and reviews every stale node. It then does two things:

1. **Updates your live feature docs** — capabilities table (status to Done, matching the cycle manifest's capabilities), design spec (if architecture changed), interface specs (if CLI/API changed), and creates doc-to-code links via `cortex_add_link`. The Auditor IS the post-implementation protocol — there is no separate step.
2. **Writes the Impact Report** — the `auditor` section in the cycle manifest, recording what was reviewed, marked clean, and flagged.

Each finding in the Impact Report is categorized as:
- **Expected** — the node is in the change-set (planned by the Architect or in files the Builder actually changed). Direct result of this PEV cycle.
- **Collateral** — the node is stale but not in the change-set (from external merges between sessions, indirect effects, or unrelated changes). Flagged for awareness; you decide whether to address or defer.

The Auditor is the only agent that creates `cortex_add_link` edges between docs and code. The Architect references node IDs for planning, but the Auditor creates the actual graph edges after verifying the code exists.

The Auditor also checks for logging gaps, test quality, and workflow markers per the Auditor Reference Protocol.

For each stale node, the Auditor either marks it clean + removes the tag (two separate actions), or flags it for a fix.

**What you see:** An Impact Report showing what docs were updated, what was marked clean (with expected/collateral categorization), and what needs fixing.

**Your options:**
- **Approve** — everything is committed in a single commit (code + docs together)
- **Request fixes** — specific items go back to the Builder for targeted fixes, then the Auditor re-checks

sections.heading: The Cycle Manifest
sections.content:
Every PEV session creates a cycle manifest doc at `docs/pev-cycles/{cycle-id}.json`. This is the single source of truth for the session's lifecycle. The cycle ID is a slugified version of your request (e.g., `pev-2026-03-21-add-history-filtering`).

### What's In It

| Section | Who writes it | When | What it contains |
|---|---|---|---|
| `status` | Orchestrator | Every phase transition | Current phase (`planning`, `builder`, `auditor`, `commit-ready`, `completed`, `incomplete`), `baseline_sha` (main HEAD at cycle start), timestamps |
| `request` | Orchestrator | Session start | Your original prompt, verbatim |
| `scope` | Architect | Planning phase | Which cortex nodes are affected and what changes are planned |
| `architect` | Architect | Planning complete | Target state design, decisions, rationale |
| `change-set` | Orchestrator | After Builder merge | Planned nodes + actual files changed + deviations. The Auditor uses this to distinguish expected vs collateral staleness. |
| `builder` | Orchestrator (from Builder) | Implementation complete | Implementation manifest: deviations from spec, files changed, tests added. The Builder returns this; the orchestrator writes it. |
| `auditor` | Auditor | Validation complete | Impact report: what was reviewed, marked clean (expected/collateral), flagged for fix |

### Lifecycle

```
/pev "Add history filtering"
  │
  ├─ User confirms cycle identity
  │
  ├─ Orchestrator creates: docs/pev-cycles/pev-2026-03-21-add-history-filtering.json
  │    status: planning, baseline_sha: abc1234, request: "Add history filtering"
  │    tagged: pev-active
  │
  ├─ Architect fills: scope, architect sections
  │    → Human approves plan
  │    → Status updated to: builder (immediately on approval)
  │
  ├─ Orchestrator creates worktree, runs cortex_checkout (VACUUM INTO)
  │
  ├─ Builder returns: implementation manifest
  │    → Orchestrator writes builder section
  │    → Human approves code
  │    → git merge, cortex build + check on main
  │    → Orchestrator constructs change-set section
  │    → Status updated to: auditor
  │
  ├─ Auditor fills: auditor section (impact report)
  │    Reads change-set to categorize expected vs collateral
  │    Updates live feature docs (capabilities, design spec, links)
  │    → Human approves
  │    → Status updated to: commit-ready
  │
  ├─ Orchestrator updates status: completed (+ commit SHA)
  │    Removes pev-active tag
  │    Single commit: code + docs together
  │
  Done.
```

### Resuming an Interrupted Session

If you cancel mid-session (Escape, crash, timeout), the cycle manifest persists with `pev-active` tag. Next time you run `/pev`:

```
/pev
  "Active PEV cycles found:
    1. pev-2026-03-21-add-history-filtering: 'Add history filtering' — phase: builder
   Resume, release, or start new?"
```

- **Resume** — picks up at the interrupted phase, skipping completed ones. The orchestrator checks both the `status` section and which sections have content to determine exactly where to resume. For example: if status is `builder` but the `builder` section is empty, the Builder hasn't started yet.
- **Release** — marks the cycle `incomplete`, removes `pev-active` tag
- **Start new** — creates a fresh cycle (the old one stays as `incomplete` for reference)

### Parallel Sessions

You can run multiple PEV cycles at the same time (e.g., two agents working on different features). Each gets its own cycle manifest doc. They don't interfere because:

- Each Architect writes to its own manifest, not to shared feature docs (enforced by Layer 2 hook that checks `doc_id`)
- Each Builder works in its own git worktree (orchestrator-created)
- Code conflicts are handled by git merge when worktrees merge back
- The Auditor updates feature docs on main after merge — the orchestrator enforces sequential dispatch (checks for other active Auditors before dispatching)
- `cortex build` + `cortex check` catch any staleness after merge
- Each cycle's `baseline_sha` tracks where it started, so diffs are always scoped correctly

sections.heading: Understanding Tags
sections.content:
During a PEV session, the cycle manifest doc gets tags showing cycle status:

| Tag | Set by | Meaning | What to do |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pev-active` | Orchestrator | This cycle is currently running | Nothing — it's in progress |
| `planned` | Architect | Scope identified, not yet built | Nothing — the Builder will work on it next |
| `in_progress` | Orchestrator | Builder is actively implementing | Nothing — wait for the Builder to finish |
| `incomplete` | Orchestrator | The cycle was interrupted or failed | Decide: resume via `/pev`, or start fresh |
| *(no tag)* | Auditor removes | Cycle completed, all nodes verified | Done |

Tags live on the cycle manifest doc, not on shared feature docs. This means parallel PEV cycles have completely independent tag sets — no contention. Each Architect's doc-write scope is structurally restricted to its own cycle manifest (Layer 2 hook).

Tags are visible in the viz dashboard and queryable via `cortex_list(tag="...")`. After a successful PEV session, all tags should be removed from the cycle manifest.

sections.heading: Reading the Impact Report
sections.content:
After the Auditor finishes, you get an Impact Report with four sections:

**docs_updated** — Doc sections the Auditor changed to match the code. Review these to make sure the updates are accurate.

**marked_clean** — Nodes the Auditor reviewed and confirmed are correct. Each has a reason explaining why it's fine.

**needs_fix** — Problems the Auditor found but cannot fix (it can't write code). Each has a category:
- `code_bug` — actual code issue
- `needs_new_tests` — changed code with no test coverage
- `test_budget` — too many tests for the scope of change
- `logging` — missing or outdated logging per ADR-014
- `workflow_markers` — stale or missing dFlow step markers

**tags_removed** — Doc nodes that completed the full cycle (mark_clean + tag removal).

If `needs_fix` has items, the orchestrator can loop back to the Builder for targeted fixes (max 2 times). After that, remaining issues are surfaced to you.

sections.heading: When Things Go Wrong
sections.content:
**Builder fails or times out:** The orchestrator updates the cycle manifest status to `incomplete`. The `pev-active` tag stays — it means "this cycle hasn't been closed out." You can resume later — the next `/pev` invocation will find the incomplete cycle via the `pev-active` tag and offer to pick up where it left off.

**Merge conflict after Builder:** The orchestrator shows you which files conflict. It never auto-resolves — you decide how to handle it.

**Auditor can't resolve staleness:** After 2 loopback iterations, remaining `needs_fix` items are shown to you for manual resolution. The Auditor has done what it can.

**Mid-session abort (Escape):** The cycle manifest and `pev-active` tag persist. On your next `/pev`, the orchestrator will show you the interrupted cycle and ask whether to resume, release, or start fresh. No manual tag cleanup needed.

**Long-running agent:** If an agent's context grows large (many files read, many nodes reviewed), a deterministic hook automatically checkpoints its progress and the orchestrator deploys a fresh agent to continue. This is seamless — the fresh agent reads the checkpoint from the cycle manifest and picks up where the previous one left off. Builder incarnations reuse the same worktree, so code on disk is preserved. You'll see a note in the cycle manifest's status section tracking incarnation count.

**Parallel cycle conflict:** If two Builders edit the same code file, git merge handles it — you'll see a merge conflict to resolve. If two Auditors need to update the same feature doc section, the orchestrator enforces sequential dispatch — the second waits for the first to complete.
